body_txt: This page is about the trope. For the 1963 novel by John Fowles or the film based on it, go here . For the 1967 movie, go here . For the Canadian TV series, go here . For the 2009 movie, go here . For the character from The Owl House , go here . The Collector is a character archetype driven by a desire to collect... things. Often, he doesn't really have a theme beyond grabbing whoever and whatever catches his eye, but some Collectors have more specific interests, such as artifacts from turning points in history, the setting's Cosmic Keystones , the last members of endangered species, great heroes or villains, or the like. Collectors usually live in seclusion, often on a hidden starship or secret base, where they can curate their collections in peace. The hoard created by this character can include almost anything, but common sights will include Legendary Weapons , Lost Technology , Precursor artifacts, and other incredibly precious and legendary items. A living creatures section is also often present, with little regard for the sapience of the exhibits; this will operate like a mixture of a Monster Menagerie and a People Zoo . When a character runs afoul of this figure, they'll decide to add them to their collection. Usually this is seen in Speculative Fiction : the collector is an alien, often of the sufficiently advanced variety, and the collection is only revealed at the end in a Twist Ending . But he could just as easily be a slaver or an obsessed lunatic in a present or past setting. He's prone to saying " It Must Be Mine! " and " If I Can't Have You… , no one can!" In other cases, characters may need to burgle a special artifact out of these figures' hoards, or else may come to seek them out on purpose in order to hide away a dangerous artifact that they can't allow to be used, counting on the Collector's greed and powerful security to safeguard something that they can't. Where the horror of being added to a collection is not the point of a Twist Ending , then the remaining cast members mobilize to rescue the kidnapped individual — and usually free all the other "specimens" in the collection in the process. Often a Collector of the Strange . A variation sometimes seen in children's shows has the collector stealing the character's unusual/unique pet. Vulnerable to Interrogation by Vandalism . If they have somewhat altruistic motives, their "collection" will be a comfortable Fantastic Nature Reserve . Of course, it may just be a Gilded Cage ... compare Living Doll Collector and Living Museum Exhibit . A Nerd Hoard is a typically non-villainous version, but overlap is possible.
